questions.stem: The magnetic force on a charge moving in a field is $F = qvB\sin\theta$. A charge of $2.0\ \mu\text{C}$ moves at $1000\ \text{m/s}$ perpendicular to a magnetic field of $0.30\ \text{T}$. What is the magnitude of the force on it?
questions.explanation: With the velocity perpendicular to the field, $\sin 90^\circ = 1$, so $F = qvB = (2.0 \times 10^{-6})(1000)(0.30) = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{N}$. The exponent arithmetic is $10^{-6} \times 10^{3} = 10^{-3}$, times $0.30$ gives $6.0 \times 10^{-4}$. Forgetting the micro prefix shifts the answer to $6.0 \times 10^{-1}\ \text{N}$. The trap is $6.0 \times 10^{-1}\ \text{N}$, which drops the $10^{-6}$ factor of the charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charged particle moves through a magnetic field with its velocity exactly parallel to the field lines. What is the magnetic force on it?
options: Maximum, because the particle moves along the field
questions.explanation: The force is $F = qvB\sin\theta$, and when the velocity is parallel to the field, $\theta = 0$ and $\sin 0^\circ = 0$, so the force vanishes. The magnetic force is greatest when the motion is perpendicular to the field, not parallel. The magnetic force is always perpendicular to the velocity, never along it. The trap is the maximum option, which confuses parallel motion (zero force) with perpendicular motion (maximum force).
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: The force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field is $F = BIL\sin\theta$. A straight wire of length $2.0\ \text{m}$ carries a current of $4.0\ \text{A}$ perpendicular to a field of $0.50\ \text{T}$. What is the force on the wire?
questions.explanation: Perpendicular orientation gives $\sin 90^\circ = 1$, so $F = BIL = (0.50)(4.0)(2.0) = 4.0\ \text{N}$. All three factors simply multiply together. Dividing somewhere gives $0.25\ \text{N}$, and $8.0\ \text{N}$ doubles the result. The trap is $8.0\ \text{N}$, which mistakenly doubles one of the factors, perhaps the length.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: What is the SI unit of magnetic flux density (magnetic field strength) $B$?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of magnetic flux density is the tesla, where $1\ \text{T} = 1\ \text{N}/(\text{A m})$. The weber is the unit of magnetic flux $\Phi$, equal to a tesla times a square metre, a different quantity. The henry is the unit of inductance. The gauss is a non-SI unit ($10^{-4}\ \text{T}$). The trap is the weber, which measures total flux rather than flux density.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: An electron (charge magnitude $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$) moves at $2.0 \times 10^{6}\ \text{m/s}$ perpendicular to a magnetic field of $0.50\ \text{T}$. What is the magnitude of the magnetic force on it?
questions.explanation: Compute $F = qvB = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(2.0 \times 10^{6})(0.50)$. First $1.6 \times 2.0 \times 0.50 = 1.6$, and the exponents give $10^{-19} \times 10^{6} = 10^{-13}$, so $F = 1.6 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{N}$. The answer is a force in newtons, not a charge in coulombs, so the unit must read N. Forgetting the factor $0.50$ gives $3.2 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{N}$. The trap is the option with units of coulombs, which keeps the right number but the wrong physical quantity.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: The magnetic field at distance $r$ from a long straight wire carrying current $I$ is $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$, where $\dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi} = 2.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{T m/A}$. What is the field $0.050\ \text{m}$ from a wire carrying $10\ \text{A}$?
questions.explanation: Substitute into $B = (2.0 \times 10^{-7})\dfrac{I}{r} = (2.0 \times 10^{-7})\dfrac{10}{0.050} = (2.0 \times 10^{-7})(200) = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{T}$. The ratio $10/0.050 = 200$ is the key step. Using $r = 0.50\ \text{m}$ by a decimal slip gives $4.0 \times 10^{-6}$, near the $2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ option. The trap is $8.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{T}$, which doubles the result as if the $2\pi$ had been mishandled into a factor of $2$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field with its velocity perpendicular to the field. What is the shape of its path?
questions.explanation: The magnetic force is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work and the speed is constant, but it continually turns the velocity. A constant-magnitude force perpendicular to the motion produces uniform circular motion. A parabola would require a constant force in a fixed direction, as in projectile motion under gravity. The particle does not speed up, since the magnetic force does no work. The trap is the parabola, which borrows the projectile picture where the force direction is fixed.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A proton (charge $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, mass $1.67 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$) moves at $1.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m/s}$ perpendicular to a field of $0.50\ \text{T}$. What is the radius of its circular path? Give two significant figures.
questions.explanation: For circular motion the magnetic force provides the centripetal force, $qvB = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$, which rearranges to $r = \dfrac{mv}{qB} = \dfrac{(1.67 \times 10^{-27})(1.0 \times 10^{5})}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(0.50)} = \dfrac{1.67 \times 10^{-22}}{8.0 \times 10^{-20}} \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$. The exponent arithmetic gives $10^{-22}/10^{-20} = 10^{-2}$. Inverting the ratio gives the huge $4.8 \times 10^{2}\ \text{m}$. The trap is $4.8 \times 10^{2}\ \text{m}$, which computes $qB/(mv)$ upside down.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A conducting rod of length $0.20\ \text{m}$ moves at $3.0\ \text{m/s}$ perpendicular to a magnetic field of $0.50\ \text{T}$. What is the motional emf induced across the ends of the rod?
questions.explanation: The motional emf is $\varepsilon = BLv = (0.50)(0.20)(3.0) = 0.30\ \text{V}$. The three factors multiply directly when the motion is perpendicular to both the rod and the field. A power-of-ten slip in the length gives $3.0\ \text{V}$ or $0.030\ \text{V}$. The trap is $3.0\ \text{V}$, which uses $L = 2.0\ \text{m}$ by misplacing the decimal in the rod length.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Magnetic flux through a flat coil is $\Phi = BA\cos\theta$. A field of $0.20\ \text{T}$ passes straight through a flat coil of area $0.50\ \text{m}^2$, perpendicular to its plane. What is the magnetic flux through the coil?
questions.explanation: When the field is perpendicular to the plane of the coil, it is parallel to the area normal, so $\theta = 0$ and $\cos 0^\circ = 1$. Then $\Phi = BA = (0.20)(0.50) = 0.10\ \text{Wb}$. Dividing instead of multiplying gives $2.5\ \text{Wb}$, and a flux of zero would need the field to lie in the plane of the coil. The trap is $0\ \text{Wb}$, which confuses a field perpendicular to the coil plane with one parallel to it.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Faraday's law gives the induced emf in a coil as $\varepsilon = N\dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t}$ in magnitude. A coil of $100$ turns has the flux through it change by $0.020\ \text{Wb}$ in $0.10\ \text{s}$. What is the magnitude of the induced emf?
questions.explanation: Apply $\varepsilon = N\dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t} = 100 \times \dfrac{0.020}{0.10} = 100 \times 0.20 = 20\ \text{V}$. The rate of change of flux is $0.20\ \text{Wb/s}$, and multiplying by the $100$ turns gives $20\ \text{V}$. Forgetting the number of turns gives $0.20\ \text{V}$. The trap is $0.20\ \text{V}$, which uses the flux rate for a single turn and omits the factor of $N = 100$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Lenz's law states that the direction of an induced current opposes the change that produces it. As the north pole of a magnet is pushed toward a coil, what is the response of the induced current?
options: It flows so as to make the coil face the magnet with a north pole, repelling the magnet
It flows so as to make the coil face the magnet with a south pole, attracting the magnet
It flows in whichever direction increases the magnet's motion
There is no induced current until the magnet stops
questions.explanation: By Lenz's law the induced current opposes the increasing flux, so the coil sets up a magnetic field that pushes back, presenting a north pole to the incoming north pole and repelling it. If it attracted the magnet, the induced current would speed up the change and create energy from nothing, violating energy conservation. The current exists while the flux is changing, not only after the magnet stops. The trap is the attracting option, which would breach conservation of energy by aiding the motion.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A wire of length $0.50\ \text{m}$ carries a current of $5.0\ \text{A}$ at an angle of $30^\circ$ to a magnetic field of $0.40\ \text{T}$. What is the magnitude of the force on the wire?
questions.explanation: Use $F = BIL\sin\theta = (0.40)(5.0)(0.50)\sin 30^\circ = 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.50\ \text{N}$. The product $BIL = 1.0\ \text{N}$ is then scaled by $\sin 30^\circ = 0.5$. Forgetting the angle gives the full $1.0\ \text{N}$, and using $\sin 60^\circ$ by measuring the angle wrongly gives $0.87\ \text{N}$. The trap is $0.87\ \text{N}$, which uses the cosine, or equivalently $\sin 60^\circ$, instead of $\sin 30^\circ$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Does a magnetic force do work on a charged particle as it moves through a magnetic field?
options: Yes, it slows the particle down
Yes, it speeds the particle up
No, because the force is always perpendicular to the velocity
Only if the field is very strong
questions.explanation: The magnetic force $q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is always perpendicular to the velocity, and work is force times displacement along the motion, which is zero for a perpendicular force. So the magnetic force does no work and the particle's speed and kinetic energy stay constant; only its direction changes. The field strength does not alter this conclusion, since the perpendicularity holds at any strength. The trap is the speeding-up option, which forgets that a force at right angles to the motion transfers no energy.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two long parallel wires each carry current in the same direction. What is the nature of the magnetic force between them?
options: There is no force between them
The force is always perpendicular to both wires and pushes them apart
questions.explanation: Each wire sits in the magnetic field of the other, and working out the direction of $BIL$ shows that parallel currents in the same direction attract. Antiparallel currents (opposite directions) repel, the opposite case. There is certainly a force, since each current feels the other's field. The trap is the repel option, which swaps the same-direction and opposite-direction cases.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A coil of $50$ turns and area $0.020\ \text{m}^2$ sits in a magnetic field that changes at a rate of $\dfrac{\Delta B}{\Delta t} = 0.50\ \text{T/s}$, with the field perpendicular to the coil. What is the magnitude of the induced emf?
questions.explanation: When only the field changes, $\varepsilon = NA\dfrac{\Delta B}{\Delta t} = (50)(0.020)(0.50) = 0.50\ \text{V}$. Here $NA = 1.0\ \text{m}^2$, scaled by the rate $0.50\ \text{T/s}$. Forgetting the area gives $25\ \text{V}$, and forgetting the turns gives $0.010\ \text{V}$. The trap is $0.010\ \text{V}$, which uses the area and rate for a single turn and omits the factor of $N = 50$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is $B = \mu_0 n I$, where $n$ is the number of turns per metre and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{T m/A}$. What is the field inside a solenoid with $1000$ turns per metre carrying $2.0\ \text{A}$? Give two significant figures.
questions.explanation: Substitute $B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1000)(2.0) = (1.26 \times 10^{-6})(2000) \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{T}$. The product $nI = 2000$, times $\mu_0 \approx 1.26 \times 10^{-6}$, gives $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$. Dropping the factor of $2$ from the current gives $1.3 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{T}$. The trap is $1.3 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{T}$, which uses $I = 1.0\ \text{A}$ instead of $2.0\ \text{A}$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A magnet is held stationary inside a coil, with no relative motion between them. What is the induced emf in the coil?
options: Equal to the magnet's field strength
Maximum, because the magnet is fully inside
Zero, because the flux is not changing
Equal to $BLv$ with $v = 0$ giving a constant nonzero value
questions.explanation: Induction depends on a changing flux, $\varepsilon = N\,\Delta\Phi/\Delta t$. With the magnet stationary the flux through the coil is constant, so $\Delta\Phi = 0$ and the emf is zero. A steady, unchanging flux induces nothing, no matter how strong it is. The motional formula $BLv$ with $v = 0$ correctly gives zero, not a nonzero constant. The trap is the maximum option, which wrongly ties the emf to the amount of flux rather than its rate of change.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A current of $2.0\ \text{A}$ flows in a long straight wire. At what distance from the wire is the magnetic field equal to $4.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{T}$? Take $\dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi} = 2.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{T m/A}$.
questions.explanation: Rearrange $B = (2.0 \times 10^{-7})\dfrac{I}{r}$ to $r = (2.0 \times 10^{-7})\dfrac{I}{B} = (2.0 \times 10^{-7})\dfrac{2.0}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}}$. The fraction $\dfrac{2.0}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.0 \times 10^{4}$, and times $2.0 \times 10^{-7}$ gives $1.0 \times 10^{-2} = 0.010\ \text{m}$. A single power-of-ten slip gives $0.10\ \text{m}$ or $0.0010\ \text{m}$. The trap is $0.10\ \text{m}$, which mishandles one power of ten in the exponent arithmetic.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A bar magnet is dropped through a vertical copper tube. Compared with free fall, how does it move, and why?
options: It stops completely inside the tube
It falls more slowly, because eddy currents induced in the tube oppose its motion
It falls exactly as in free fall, because copper is non-magnetic
It falls faster, because the copper attracts the magnet downward
questions.explanation: As the magnet falls, the changing flux induces eddy currents in the copper tube. By Lenz's law these currents oppose the change, exerting a retarding force on the magnet, so it falls more slowly than in free fall. Copper is not ferromagnetic, but it is a conductor, so eddy currents still arise even though there is no static attraction. The magnet slows but does not stop, since gravity still acts. The trap is the free-fall option, which assumes a non-magnetic metal cannot affect the magnet, overlooking induced eddy currents.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charge of $2.0\ \mu\text{C}$ moves at $1000\ \text{m/s}$ perpendicular to a field of $0.30\ \text{T}$. Find the magnetic force and the formula used.
questions.solution: The force on a moving charge is $F = qvB\sin\theta$; the angle factor matters because {{1}}. Here the motion is perpendicular to the field, so the force takes its {{2}}, and $F = qvB$ evaluates to {{3}} $\times 10^{-4}$ N.

options: a charge moving along the field feels no magnetic force
the charge always feels the full force regardless of direction
the angle changes only the direction, not the size
gaps.answer: a charge moving along the field feels no magnetic force

questions.explanation: The magnetic force is $qvB\sin\theta$; a charge moving parallel to the field ($\theta = 0$) feels nothing, while perpendicular motion ($\sin 90^\circ = 1$) gives the maximum. Here $F = (2.0\times10^{-6})(1000)(0.30) = 6\times10^{-4}$ N.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A straight wire of length $2.0\ \text{m}$ carries $4.0\ \text{A}$ perpendicular to a field of $0.50\ \text{T}$. Find the force and the SI unit of magnetic field.
questions.solution: The force on a current-carrying wire perpendicular to the field is $F = BIL$, which evaluates to {{1}} N. The SI unit of the magnetic flux density $B$ is the {{2}}, which in base terms is the same as {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $F = BIL = (0.50)(4.0)(2.0) = 4$ N. $B$ is measured in tesla; the weber measures flux and the henry inductance. Since $B = F/(IL)$, one tesla is one N/(A m).
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A long straight wire carries $10\ \text{A}$. Take $\dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi} = 2.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{T m/A}$. Find the field at $0.050\ \text{m}$ and state how it changes if the distance doubles.
questions.solution: The field of a long straight wire is $B = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\frac{I}{r}$, so $B$ depends on the distance as {{1}}. Evaluating at $r = 0.050$ m gives $B =$ {{2}} $\times 10^{-5}$ T. Doubling the distance therefore {{3}} the field.
questions.explanation: A wire's field falls off as $1/r$ (not $1/r^2$, which is the point-charge law, the classic mix-up). $B = (2.0\times10^{-7})(10/0.050) = 4\times10^{-5}$ T; doubling $r$ halves it.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A rod of length $0.20\ \text{m}$ moves at $3.0\ \text{m/s}$ perpendicular to a field of $0.50\ \text{T}$. Find the motional emf and the law it illustrates.
questions.solution: A rod moving across a field develops a motional emf $\varepsilon = BLv$, which evaluates to {{1}} V. This induction of an emf by relative motion is an instance of {{2}}, and the direction of the induced current is fixed by {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\varepsilon = BLv = (0.50)(0.20)(3.0) = 0.3$ V. Faraday's law governs the size of an induced emf; Lenz's law fixes its direction so the induced current opposes the change.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A coil of $100$ turns has its flux change by $0.020\ \text{Wb}$ in $0.10\ \text{s}$. Find the induced emf and state the role of the number of turns.
questions.solution: The emf induced in a coil is $\varepsilon = N\frac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t}$, so the number of turns enters as a {{1}}. Substituting gives $\varepsilon =$ {{2}} V. If the same flux change happened in half the time, the emf would instead {{3}}.

options: direct multiplier, so more turns give more emf
divisor, so more turns reduce emf
quantity that does not affect the emf
gaps.answer: direct multiplier, so more turns give more emf

questions.explanation: $\varepsilon = N\Delta\Phi/\Delta t = 100(0.020/0.10) = 20$ V. Turns multiply the emf; and since $\varepsilon \propto 1/\Delta t$, halving the time doubles the emf.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A proton (charge $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, mass $1.67 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$) moves at $1.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m/s}$ perpendicular to a $0.50\ \text{T}$ field. Find the radius of its circular path and state what provides the centripetal force.
questions.solution: A charged particle in a perpendicular field moves in a circle because the magnetic force acts as the {{1}}. Setting $qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$ and solving gives $r = \frac{mv}{qB}$, which evaluates to {{2}} $\times 10^{-3}$ m. If the particle's speed doubled, the radius would {{3}}.

options: centripetal force, always perpendicular to the velocity
a force along the velocity that speeds the particle up
a force that does work on the particle
an outward force pushing it away
gaps.answer: centripetal force, always perpendicular to the velocity

questions.explanation: The magnetic force is always perpendicular to $v$, so it does no work and only bends the path into a circle: it is the centripetal force. $r = mv/(qB) = (1.67\times10^{-27})(10^5)/[(1.6\times10^{-19})(0.50)] \approx 2.1\times10^{-3}$ m, and $r \propto v$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers
